summary: DHH announces the first RailsConf, scheduled for June 2006, projecting around 400 attendees at $350-400 per ticket. He frames the conference as a milestone that makes the Rails ecosystem feel tangible and larger than any individual contributor. Beyond community building, he positions it as a proof of concept to demonstrate market viability to potential vendors and companies showing interest in Rails. He expresses excitement about moving beyond introductory-level content to serious, in-depth technical conversations.
key_points: The first RailsConf is announced for June 22-25, 2006, just before Rails' two-year anniversary
DHH sees the conference as validation that Rails has grown beyond any single person into a real ecosystem
Getting 400 paying attendees at $350-400 each would serve as a commercial proof of concept for vendors eyeing the Rails market
DHH is eager to move past introductory 'how to make a weblog' content toward deeper technical discussions
money_quotes: Announcing RailsConf makes the ecosystem seem so real.
This whole Rails thing is bigger than anyone person, bigger than me, bigger than the core team, bigger than the circle of people that I know doing it.
That's a great feeling. It's the feeling of creation.
If we can actually get some 400 people together who are willing to shell out around $350-400, that's a strong signal to potential vendors that there is a market here.
Going beyond the bland introduction of 'how to make a weblog' should provide for a welcome change of scenery.
key_insight: A community conference serves as both an emotional milestone proving an open source project has transcended its creator and a commercial signal proving market viability to the broader industry.
